message:
security(replay): bound .war recording size to 256 MiB on load

## Why

Recording::load currently reads the whole .war file into memory
unconditionally. A hostile or accidentally-huge recording — e.g. one
planted in a cloned repo, dropped into a shared replay directory, or
produced by a runaway recorder — would OOM the process the first time
a user runs `ft replay <path>`. There's no legitimate reason for a
frankenterm recording to be multi-gigabyte; typical sessions are
single-digit MB.

## Change

Adds `MAX_RECORDING_BYTES = 256 * 1024 * 1024` (256 MiB) as a public
const and gates Recording::load on it:

  - Stat the path first via std::fs::metadata.
  - If the file length exceeds MAX_RECORDING_BYTES, return a Runtime
    error that names the observed length and the ceiling, so the user
    knows *why* the load was refused.
  - Only if the size check passes do we actually open + read the file
    into memory.

256 MiB was chosen because it comfortably exceeds any realistic
recording we've seen in practice while still being small enough that
a malicious .war can't wedge a user's box.

Includes a test (recording_load_rejects_oversized_file) that uses
std::fs::File::set_len to produce a sparse file one byte over the
ceiling and asserts load() refuses it.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
